policy_statement: Review and upgrade flood proofing measures in City-owned buildings in the regulatory floodplain where possible.
mechanism_description: The city reviews and upgrades flood-proofing measures in city-owned buildings in flood-prone areas to reduce flooding impacts and damage.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (flooding) → ↓ exposure/vulnerability of buildings → ↓ physical damage + ↓ downtime/impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed system/activity: The city assesses and retrofits city-owned buildings in flood-prone areas with flood-proofing upgrades (e.g., barriers, dry/wet floodproofing, elevation of critical equipment, backflow prevention, sealing, pumps, site drainage improvements).  
(2) Mediating physical process: Floodwater ingress depth/duration and hydrostatic/hydrodynamic impacts on structures and building systems are reduced; critical components are protected so failure thresholds are less likely to be exceeded.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower expected losses, faster recovery, and improved continuity of public services under increased flood frequency/severity associated with climate change.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced damage and service disruption from flooding in buildings during extreme precipitation/riverine flood events.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Flood hazard acting on buildings is managed by reducing vulnerability/exposure through flood-proofing, yielding reduced impacts—an adaptation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While retrofits can incidentally affect energy use or embodied emissions, the intent and dominant effect here is damage reduction from flooding, not emissions mitigation or resource efficiency.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “flood-proofing upgrade” were implemented mainly as part of deep energy retrofits or low-carbon rebuilding, that would introduce mitigation co-benefits, but the mechanism as defined remains impact reduction from flooding.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy fitting “building flood-proofing upgrades → flood damage reduction” is fundamentally about reducing flood impacts on the built environment, which is adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment: Specific adaptation subtype (e.g., critical facilities resilience, property protection, continuity planning) depends on policy-level details and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Flood-proofing can sometimes increase energy use (e.g., added pumps/dehumidification) or trigger reconstruction with embodied emissions; these are secondary effects and do not change the dominant adaptation classification.
key_indicators: “Review and upgrade flood proofing measures”; “City-owned buildings”; “regulatory floodplain”
co_benefits: Potential protection of critical public services/operations continuity; reduced repair/insurance costs; improved public safety during flood events
instance_edge_case_notes: “Review” could be interpreted as planning/assessment, but paired with “upgrade” it implies implementation of physical retrofits; no explicit budget mentioned, yet the instrument remains a direct asset upgrade/investment action.
